question: Explain the concept of a stack data structure. Describe its two fundamental operations, including how they work and their common names.
expectedPoints: Definition: A LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) abstract data type.
Operation 1: Push - adds an element to the top of the stack.
Operation 2: Pop - removes and returns the element from the top of the stack.
Brief real-world analogy (e.g., a stack of plates).

question: Compare and contrast arrays and linked lists as fundamental data structures. Discuss their key differences in terms of memory allocation, access time (for random access and insertion/deletion), and typical use cases.
expectedPoints: Memory: Arrays use contiguous memory; linked lists use non-contiguous, dynamic memory.
Access: Arrays have O(1) random access; linked lists have O(n) access.
Insertion/Deletion: Arrays are inefficient (O(n)); linked lists are efficient at head/tail (O(1)) if a pointer is maintained.
Use Cases: Arrays for frequent access; linked lists for frequent insertions/deletions and unknown size.

question: Explain the process of inserting a new node into a Binary Search Tree (BST). Detail the steps of the insertion algorithm, including how it traverses the tree and maintains the BST property. Provide a small example to illustrate the process.
expectedPoints: BST Property: Left child < Parent < Right child.
Algorithm: Start at root, compare new key, traverse left if smaller, right if larger, until a null pointer is found.
Insertion: Attach the new node at the null pointer location.
Example: Inserting a value (e.g., 15) into a given small BST and showing the path taken (e.g., root->20->left->10->right->null).
Note: The operation's average time complexity is O(log n) for a balanced tree.
